Skills lost: words, social engagement, eye contact, play skills, self-care abilities. **NOT your fault** - caused by neurological changes, not parenting. **Medical evaluation URGENT** to rule out treatable causes.

**Medical Rule-Outs IMMEDIATE:** 1) EEG (seizure activity can cause regression), 2) Metabolic testing, 3) Genetic testing (some syndromes show regression pattern), 4) Hearing/vision assessment, 5) PANDAS/PANS evaluation (autoimmune reaction to infection), 6) Lead poisoning screen. **Some causes reversible** with treatment - don't delay.

**Common Regression Triggers:** 1) Seizure activity (subclinical, not obvious convulsions), 2) Significant stressors (new sibling, moving, divorce, death), 3) Medical illness (high fever, hospitalization), 4) Sensory overwhelm (environmental changes), 5) Unknown neurological factors. **Identifying trigger guides intervention.**

**Skill Recovery Strategies:** 1) Increase therapy intensity (speech, OT, ABA), 2) Return to basics (treat child as younger temporarily), 3) Visual supports for all routines, 4) Reduce demands/stress while rebuilding, 5) Document EVERY small gain (photos, videos). **Progress slow but possible** - many children regain lost skills with intensive support.

**Emotional Support for Parents:** Watching your child regress is traumatic, devastating. Allow yourself to grieve. Connect with other parents who understand. Therapy helps process loss. **Important:** Regression doesn't mean permanent loss. Many children recover and surpass previous levels. Your advocacy and intensive intervention make difference. **Hope is real.**

**Using local materials:** Regression can be triggered by illness, family stress, or major changes like moving or a new sibling. Rule out physical illness first at your nearest health center. Re-establish familiar routines using visual cues. Contact Autism Rwanda if regression is severe or prolonged.

When your child loses skills they had before, it is frightening. First, check for illness - fever, ear infection, stomach pain, constipation. Many regressions are caused by physical pain the child cannot tell you about. Go to the health center. Second, check for big changes - new baby, moving house, death in family, change of school. These can cause regression. Third, go back to basics: same routine, same foods, same sleep time. Rebuild the foundation. The skills often come back. Be patient. You have not lost your child.

**SEIZURE FIRST AID - STAY CALM:** 1) Time the seizure (note start time), 2) Clear area of dangerous objects, 3) Cushion head with folded clothing, 4) Turn on side (prevents choking), 5) Loosen tight clothing around neck. **DON'T:** Restrain movements, put anything in mouth, give food/water/meds until fully alert. **Stay with child** until fully conscious.

**CALL 114 (Rwanda emergency) IF:** 1) Seizure lasts >5 minutes, 2) Repeated seizures without recovery, 3) First-time seizure, 4) Difficulty breathing afterwards, 5) Injury during seizure, 6) Seizure in water, 7) Child has diabetes/heart condition. **When in doubt, call** - better safe than sorry.

**Types of Seizures in Autism:** 1) Tonic-clonic (convulsions, loss consciousness), 2) Absence (staring spells, unresponsive 5-15 sec), 3) Myoclonic (sudden jerks), 4) Atonic (drop attacks, sudden limpness). **Absence seizures often missed** - looks like daydreaming. If frequent staring spells, request EEG evaluation.

**Seizure Triggers:** Common triggers: 1) Sleep deprivation (MAJOR trigger), 2) Stress/excitement, 3) Flashing/strobing lights (some individuals), 4) Illness/fever, 5) Missed medications, 6) Hormonal changes (menstruation). **Track patterns** in seizure diary to identify YOUR child's triggers.

**Medical Management:** 1) Pediatric neurologist specializing in epilepsy, 2) EEG to confirm diagnosis/type, 3) MRI to rule out structural causes, 4) Anti-seizure medications (ASMs) - multiple options, 5) Regular blood monitoring (medication levels, liver function). **Medication controls seizures in 70%** but may take trials to find right one. **Never stop abruptly** - can trigger status epilepticus (life-threatening).

**Using local materials:** Epilepsy medication (like phenobarbital) is available at district hospitals under Mutuelle de Sante. Keep a seizure diary to show the doctor. Inform the school and community health worker about your child's seizures. Never leave a child with known seizures near water unsupervised.

patterns: won't use toilet for bowel movements
responses: **Readiness Signs:** Don't rush - wait for readiness: 1) Staying dry 2+ hours, 2) Awareness of need (hiding, grunting, holding), 3) Can follow simple instructions, 4) Discomfort in soiled diaper, 5) Motor skills (pull pants up/down). **Autistic children often train later** (age 4-7 common, sometimes older). **Not race** - readiness matters most.

**Step-by-Step Training:** 1) Establish routine (sit every 2 hours, after meals, before bed), 2) Use visual schedule (pants down â†’ sit â†’ wipe â†’ flush â†’ wash), 3) Timer reminders (vibrating watch), 4) Immediate reinforcement (praise + small treat for success), 5) Accidents = neutral cleanup ('Oops, pee goes in toilet'). **No punishment** - creates anxiety.

**Sensory Bathroom Modifications:** 1) Footstool for stability (feet flat = easier elimination), 2) Reduce flush noise (leave room before flushing, use ear defenders), 3) Comfortable seat (not cold/hard - add cushion), 4) Good lighting (not shadowy/scary), 5) Pleasant smells (essential oils, good ventilation). **Make bathroom welcoming, not terrifying.**

**Constipation Management:** Major cause of accidents. 1) High-fiber diet (fruits, vegetables, whole grains), 2) Adequate water intake, 3) Regular physical activity, 4) Scheduled toilet sits after meals (gastrocolic reflex), 5) Stool softeners if pediatrician recommends. **Chronic constipation requires medical treatment** - doesn't resolve alone.

**Bedwetting (Enuresis):** Common in autism even into teens. 1) Limit fluids 2 hours before bed, 2) Toilet right before sleep, 3) Waterproof mattress protector, 4) Consider bedwetting alarm (moisture sensor wakes child), 5) Medication options (consult doctor for special occasions). **Never shame** - involuntary, not laziness. Many children outgrow naturally.

**Using local materials:** Use a consistent toilet spot and time (after meals, morning, evening). A small stool for foot support helps children feel secure. Reward with praise or a small preferred food. If the child is over 5 and showing no progress, visit a health center to rule out physical causes. Reusable cloth training pants can be made locally.

**IMMEDIATE MEDICAL CONCERN:** Pica (eating non-food) can be fatal. 1) Schedule doctor appointment IMMEDIATELY, 2) Request blood test (lead poisoning, iron deficiency, zinc deficiency), 3) Deworm if soil-eating (parasite risk), 4) Discuss supplementation if deficient. **Common causes:** Iron deficiency, mineral deficiencies, sensory-seeking, oral stimulation needs.

**Environmental Safety:** 1) Remove ALL access to non-food items (lock cleaning supplies, pick up small objects), 2) Cover bare soil/mud (lay bricks, concrete, grass), 3) Secure pet food/litter boxes, 4) Check yard for rocks/debris before outdoor play. **Constant supervision required** until behavior controlled.

**Replacement Strategies:** 1) Provide SAFE oral input (chewelry, crunchy snacks, gum if age-appropriate), 2) Scheduled sensory breaks (heavy work, oral motor activities), 3) Teach 'spit out' command with immediate reward, 4) Visual supports: pictures of 'YES eat' vs. 'NO eat.' **Reinforce heavily** when child chooses safe items.

**Behavioral Intervention:** Track ABCs (Antecedent-Behavior-Consequence). **Pattern examples:** If boredom â†’ provide activities. If sensory-seeking â†’ offer chewy tubes. If attention â†’ give attention for appropriate eating only. **Never punish** - pica often compulsive, not defiance.

**Emergency Red Flags:** Go to ER immediately if: 1) Swallowed batteries (chemical burns), 2) Sharp objects (internal cuts), 3) Multiple magnets (intestinal perforation), 4) Lead paint chips (poisoning), 5) Large quantities of anything. **Don't wait for symptoms** - some toxins silent until severe damage.

**Using local materials:** Keep soil, charcoal, and small objects out of reach. Supervise outdoor play closely. If the child eats soil regularly, visit the health center to check for iron or zinc deficiency, which is common and treatable. Call 114 if the child swallows something dangerous like a battery or sharp object.

**What caregivers do when a child eats dirt, rocks, or other non-food:** This can kill your child. Take it seriously. First, go to the health center immediately. Ask for a blood test - your child may have iron deficiency (anemia) which causes dirt eating. Also ask for deworming medicine. Second, while waiting: cover bare soil with bricks, concrete, or grass. Remove small rocks from the yard. Lock away soap, cleaning products, and batteries. Third, give your child safe things to put in their mouth: clean cloth to chew, crunchy vegetables (carrots, raw beans - supervise), or a clean stick to hold. Fourth, teach 'spit out' - practice with safe items first. Reward heavily when they spit out non-food. Do not punish. This is not defiance. This is a medical and sensory need.

**Puberty & Masturbation:** Self-touch is normal but teach PRIVACY. 1) Define private places (bedroom, bathroom with door closed), 2) Social story: 'Touching private parts is okay ONLY in private,' 3) Redirect immediately if public: 'Hands in pants = go to bedroom,' 4) Provide stress alternatives (fidgets, exercise). **Never shame** - teach appropriate context.

**Menstruation Management:** Start preparation BEFORE first period. 1) Visual schedule for pad changing (every 3-4 hours), 2) Practice with dolls/pads, 3) Stock ample supplies, 4) Teach disposal (wrap, trash, NOT toilet), 5) Track cycles on calendar. **Using local materials:** Use reusable cloth pads if disposable expensive, wash in hot soapy water, sun-dry for sterilization.

**Personal Space Teaching:** Use concrete rules: 1) 'Arm's length distance' from non-family, 2) Hugs ONLY with permission ('Can I hug you?'), 3) Private parts = areas covered by swimsuit, 4) Good touch vs. bad touch education. **Practice:** Role-play scenarios, use hula hoop for visual boundary.

**Body Hygiene Puberty:** 1) Deodorant daily after bathing, 2) Shower/bathe daily (body odor increases), 3) Clean clothes daily, 4) Hair washing regularly, 5) Oral hygiene (brush 2x daily). **Visual checklists** essential. Make hygiene products accessible (open shelves, picture labels).

**Safety & Consent:** Autistic teens vulnerable to exploitation. Teach: 1) 'My body belongs to ME,' 2) 'Say NO to unwanted touch,' 3) 'Tell trusted adult if someone touches private parts,' 4) 'Secrets about touch = tell immediately.' **Practice saying NO** loudly. Most abusers known to family - educate about ALL contacts.

patterns: she is running how can i calm her
responses:
This is a safety emergency. Running and wandering is the most dangerous thing for an autistic child. Act on this today - not tomorrow.

Step 1 - Secure your home right now:
- Put a latch or lock HIGH on every door - above where your child can reach
- Put a bell or tied cloth on the door handle so you hear when it opens
- Block gaps in your compound fence with sticks, netting, or old sheets

Step 2 - Water is the biggest danger:
- If there is a river, well, pond, or large water container nearby - this is where to check FIRST if your child goes missing
- Empty buckets and basins when not in use
- Cover wells
- Never leave your child unsupervised near any water

Step 3 - Prepare your community:
- Tell your neighbors, the umudugudu leader, and nearby shopkeepers about your child
- Show them a recent photo
- Say: 'My child has autism. They may not answer when called. If you see them alone, please bring them home or call me.'
- Sew or embroider your phone number on the inside of your child's clothing

Step 4 - When your child goes missing:
- Check water sources FIRST - within the first 5 minutes
- Then call 114 and give them a recent photo
- Say: 'My child has autism. They may not respond to their name.'
- Do not wait. Every minute matters.

Step 5 - Teach your child:
- Practice holding your hand or fabric belt when walking outside
- Teach 'stop' as a command - practice it every day when calm
- Use a visual card with your home address and phone number

Contact Autism Rwanda for more safety planning support: +250 788 386 185

**IMMEDIATE SAFETY PRIORITY:** Elopement is life-threatening. 1) Install door/window alarms (cheap battery ones work), 2) Add locks child can't reach (high hooks, sliding bolts), 3) Fence yard if possible, 4) Remove attraction to dangers (cover pools, empty basins). **Explanation:** Many autistic children are drawn to water or run when overwhelmed. They lack road safety awareness.

**Step-by-Step Prevention:** 1) Identify triggers (overwhelm? seeking something?), 2) Teach 'stop' command with immediate reinforcement, 3) Practice safe walking (hold hands, use harness/wrist link if needed), 4) Create safe movement spaces (fenced yard, indoor trampoline). **Rwanda context:** Use thick rope/tether for hand-holding practice, create enclosed compound with fencing materials.

**Water Safety CRITICAL:** Drowning is #1 cause of death for autistic children who wander. 1) Lock all pool gates, 2) Empty buckets/basins immediately, 3) Teach swimming early (water survival skills), 4) Always supervise near water. **Many autistic children are magnetically drawn to water** - this is common autism trait.

**If Child Goes Missing - EMERGENCY PROTOCOL:** 1) Call emergency services IMMEDIATELY (don't wait 24hrs), 2) Check ALL water sources first (pools, ponds, wells - within 5 minutes), 3) Provide recent photo + description, 4) Mention autism specifically (may not respond to name, may hide), 5) Search high-interest locations (water, construction sites, animals). **First hours are critical.**

**Identification Strategies:** 1) ID bracelet with name, address, phone, 2) Sew tags into clothing inside seams, 3) Laminate card in pocket, 4) Register with local police as special needs resident. **Using local materials:** Embroider phone number on shirt tag, use waterproof marker on child's arm for outings.

**What caregivers do when a child runs away:** This is life-threatening. Act now. First, put a latch or lock high on the door where your child cannot reach. Second, put a bell on the door so you hear when it opens. Third, if you have a compound, use fencing materials - sticks, netting, old sheets - to block gaps. Fourth, if your child runs toward water (rivers, wells, buckets of water), this is very dangerous. Empty all water containers when not in use. Cover wells. Fifth, teach your child to hold your fabric belt when walking outside. Sixth, if your child goes missing, check water FIRST within 5 minutes. Then call 114. Give them a recent photo and say 'autism - may not respond to name.' Do not wait. Every minute matters.

patterns: we don't have those things here
I am in a rural area
responses: I understand you are in Rwanda and may not have the things mentioned in some responses. That is okay. I will give you what works here. Tell me what your child is doing right now and I will give you steps using what you have at home.

Many families in Rwanda face the same situation. You do not need expensive therapy to help your child. A kitenge, a quiet corner, a consistent routine, and your presence - these are powerful. What is your biggest challenge today?

You are not alone. Autism Rwanda supports families across the country, including rural areas. Call or WhatsApp them. Your district hospital can also give you a referral. What is your most urgent concern right now?

Even without specialists, you can do a great deal at home. Visual schedules made from paper, sensory tools from local materials, and consistent daily routines are all evidence-based and free. Tell me what your child is struggling with.

Rwanda has growing autism support. Contact Autism Rwanda, visit your district hospital, or speak to your umudugudu leader about connecting you to NCPD. You have more support available than you may know. What do you need help with most?

patterns: what can I do at home
what can I do without a therapist
I have no money for therapy
free ways to help my child
what can I do without money
what can I do with no resources
responses:
You do not need money to help your child today. Here is what you can do with what you already have at home:

**For calming:** Fold a heavy kitenge or traditional blanket and place it across your child's lap. The weight calms the nervous system.

**For communication:** Use pictures from old magazines or draw simple drawings on cardboard. Point to 'eat,' 'sleep,' 'water,' 'pain.'

**For routine:** Draw the day's activities on paper - wake, eat, play, bath, sleep. Show your child what comes next.

**For sensory needs:** Let your child carry water containers (not too heavy), help grind or mix food, jump rope, fold blankets. These activities give the body what it needs.

**For meltdowns:** Turn off radio. Ask others to be quiet. Dim lights by covering window with cloth. Sit nearby. Say nothing. Wait.

**You are not failing.** Love is free. Your presence is free. Your patience is free. These matter more than any expensive therapy. Start with one small change today.

No money, no therapist, no specialist nearby - you can still help your child. Start here: same wake time every day, same meal times, same bedtime. Routine is free and it is one of the most powerful autism tools that exists. Add one thing at a time. Do not try to change everything at once.

**Free tools from your home:** Sock filled with rice or sand = weighted lap pad. Cardboard with drawings = visual schedule. Heavy blanket = deep pressure. Quiet corner with a mat = calm space. Carrying water or sweeping = heavy work for sensory regulation. You already have everything you need to start.

**Community resources in Rwanda that cost little or nothing:** Community health workers (CHWs) can do home visits - ask at your health center. Autism Rwanda offers free parent support. District hospitals provide referrals at low cost with Mutuelle de Sante. NCPD can connect you with disability benefits. Ask. Keep asking. You have rights.

Consistent daily routines are one of the most powerful autism interventions and they cost nothing. Wake up, eat, play, rest, eat, play, bath, sleep - same time every day. Predictability reduces anxiety and meltdowns significantly. Start tomorrow morning.

patterns: people say it is a curse
family says I did something wrong
is this a punishment from God
I am ashamed of my child
church says it is a demon
I feel shame about my child
people say bad things about my child
responses:
Let me speak clearly to you as one Rwandan to another. Autism is not a curse. It is not an evil spirit. It is not punishment for something you did.

Autism is how your child's brain was made. Some children are born with eyes that do not see well. Some are born with legs that do not walk well. Your child was born with a brain that works differently. That is all.

You did not cause this. Not by what you ate. Not by what you did. Not by any sin.

If neighbors or family say hurtful things, they do not understand. You can say: 'My child has autism. They need help, not blame. Please do not speak about what you do not know.'

You have nothing to be ashamed of. You are a good parent. You are seeking help. That is courage.

If people at church say your child has a demon, find a different church or speak to the pastor privately. Many pastors in Rwanda now understand autism. Ask your pastor to learn. Your child needs medical support and consistent care - not exorcism. Both faith and medicine can exist together.

Your in-laws or husband may not accept the diagnosis. This is painful. You can say: 'The doctor confirmed this is autism. It is a brain difference, not a curse. There are things we can do to help our child.' Autism Rwanda can provide materials in Kinyarwanda to share with family members who do not believe.

Stigma is real and harmful, but it can change. Many communities in Rwanda have become more accepting as families speak openly. Your child deserves to be seen in public, attend school, and participate in community life. Hiding them increases isolation and slows their development.

Connect with Autism Rwanda. They will connect you with other Rwandan parents who have heard the same words you have heard - 'curse,' 'evil spirit,' 'bad parent.' Those parents will tell you: it gets better. You are not alone. Your child is a gift, not a curse.
The shame you feel is not yours to carry. Your child did not choose autism, and neither did you. You are a good parent for seeking help today. That is what good parents do.

patterns: where to get help in Rwanda
where to take autistic child Rwanda
responses:
**Rwanda Autism Resources:**

**Autism Rwanda** - WhatsApp and phone support, parent training, referrals. Phone: +250 788 386 185. Search 'Autism Rwanda' on Facebook. Ask at your district hospital for their contact.

**NCPD (National Council of Persons with Disabilities)** - Government body that helps with referrals, advocacy, and connecting you to services. Visit their office or ask your umudugudu leader for help reaching them.

**Your District Hospital** - Go to the pediatric or mental health department. Ask for an autism assessment or referral. Even small health centers can connect you to the next level.

**Mutuelle de Sante** - Covers some health services. Ask at your health center what is included for your child.

**Emergency: 114**

**School Support:** Speak to your child's headteacher. Ask for seating at the front, breaks when overwhelmed, and no punishment for autism behaviors. If refused, contact NCPD or district education officer.

You are not alone. More Rwandan parents are seeking help every day. Your child has rights. Keep asking until you find help.

**Medical Care :** Start at your nearest health center or district hospital. Ask for a referral to a developmental pediatrician. CHUK (Centre Hospitalier Universitaire de Kigali) and King Faisal Hospital in Kigali have specialists. Mutuelle de Sante covers most consultations at public facilities. Do not wait for a perfect specialist. Start with what is near you.

**Emergency contacts:** Call 114 for any emergency. Police: 112. Autism Rwanda: +250 788 386 185. CHUK: +250 788 305 000. King Faisal Hospital: +250 252 582 421. If you have no phone, run to your nearest neighbor or health center. Do not wait.

**Financial support:** Register your child with NCPD to access disability benefits through RSSB (Rwanda Social Security Board). Ensure enrollment in Mutuelle de Sante through your sector office. Ask your sector social affairs officer for help. These services exist for your family.

**Schools:** Ask your district education office about schools with Special Needs Education (SNE) programs. Rwanda's inclusive education policy means your child has the right to attend school. If a school refuses your child, that is against the law. Contact NCPD or your district education officer.
**For parents who feel alone:** Autism Rwanda has parent support groups. Other Rwandan parents who understand what you are going through are waiting to meet you. You do not have to carry this alone.

responses: **EMERGENCY : Call 114 immediately.** This is the Rwanda emergency number. Stay on the line. If you have no phone, send someone to run to the nearest neighbor or health center right now. Do not wait.

**If your child is not breathing:** Lay them on their back. Tilt their head back gently. Check for breathing. Call 114. If you are trained, begin CPR. Send someone to flag down help or carry your child to the nearest health center. Every second matters.

**If your child is having a seizure that will not stop (more than 5 minutes):** Do NOT put anything in their mouth. Turn them on their side. Clear the area of hard objects. Call 114 or carry them to the nearest health center or district hospital immediately.

**If your child swallowed something dangerous (battery, sharp object, chemical, medicine):** Do NOT make them vomit. Call 114 immediately. Go to the nearest health center or district hospital. Bring the container or object with you so the doctor knows what it was.

**Nearest help - go in this order:** Health center (centre de sante) first. District hospital second. Referral hospital (CHUK or King Faisal in Kigali) third. For autism-specific support after the emergency, call Autism Rwanda: +250 788 386 185.

**Key Rwanda Emergency Contacts:** Emergency: 114 | Police: 112 | Autism Rwanda: +250 788 386 185 | CHUK: +250 788 305 000 | King Faisal Hospital: +250 252 582 421. Write these numbers on your wall at home.
